statement.markdown: You are given a 0-1 matrix where 0 represents water and 1 represents land. The goal is to make the largest island by flipping some 0s to 1s. You can only flip 0s that are directly adjacent (horizontally or vertically) to land. You can perform this operation at most once.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains two integers, n and m, representing the number of rows and columns in the grid. The next n lines contain m integers each, representing the grid.
statement.outputFormat: The output is the area of the largest island after flipping at most one 0 to 1.

constraints.description: number of rows in the grid
number of columns in the grid

examples.explanation: The largest island has an area of 10 after flipping one 0 to 1.
The largest island has an area of 6 after flipping one 0 to 1.

hints.text: The problem involves a grid where you can modify some water cells to land cells under certain conditions. Think about how you can identify and store the land cells and their adjacent water cells.
To maximize the size of the island, you should consider using a flood fill or similar approach to identify connected land cells. Also, keep track of the adjacent water cells for each land cell.

The key to solving this problem is to perform a breadth-first search (BFS) or depth-first search (DFS) to find all the land cells and their adjacent water cells. Then, consider which of these water cells, when flipped, would result in the largest connected component of land cells.

editorial.title: Making a Large Island: A Greedy Approach with DFS
editorial.intuition: The problem can be solved by first identifying all the islands in the grid and storing their sizes. Then, for each empty cell, we find the adjacent islands and calculate the maximum area that can be obtained by connecting them.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve checking all possible combinations of cells to form an island. However, this approach is impractical due to its exponential time complexity.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves using a Depth-First Search (DFS) to identify and size all the islands in the grid, then iterating over all empty cells to find the maximum area that can be obtained by connecting adjacent islands.
editorial.optimalSolution: The solution has a time complexity of O(n*m) and a space complexity of O(n*m), where n and m are the dimensions of the grid. This is because we visit each cell at most twice: once during the DFS and once during the iteration over empty cells.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proven by observing that the DFS correctly identifies and sizes all the islands, and that the iteration over empty cells correctly calculates the maximum area that can be obtained by connecting adjacent islands.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the solution on a sample grid can help verify its correctness and illustrate its steps.
commonMistakes: Not considering the case where an empty cell is adjacent to multiple islands.
Not keeping track of the maximum area that can be obtained by connecting adjacent islands.
Not using a DFS to efficiently identify and size all the islands in the grid.
